## Overview

In this exercise I learnt two relatively new statistical graphic methods for visualising distribution — ridgeline plots and raincloud plots — using `ggridges` and `ggdist`.

## Getting Started

### Loading packages


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
pacman::p_load(ggdist, ggridges, ggthemes,
               colorspace, tidyverse)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```

The downloaded binary packages are in
	/var/folders/dj/gj3ytvh900z09pcdgsdwpm900000gn/T//RtmpIQH407/downloaded_packages
```


### Importing data


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
exam <- read_csv("Exam_data.csv")
```
:::


## Visualising Distribution with Ridgeline Plot

A ridgeline plot (also called a Joyplot) reveals the distribution of a numeric value for several groups. It works best when there are 5 or more groups with a clear pattern or ranking.

### Basic ridgeline plot

::: panel-tabset
## The plot


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Hands-on_Ex04a_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-3-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## The code chunk


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(exam,
       aes(x = ENGLISH,
           y = CLASS)) +
  geom_density_ridges(
    scale = 3,
    rel_min_height = 0.01,
    bandwidth = 3.4,
    fill = lighten("#7097BB", .3),
    color = "white"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    name = "English grades",
    expand = c(0, 0)
    ) +
  scale_y_discrete(name = NULL, expand = expansion(add = c(0.2, 2.6))) +
  theme_ridges()
```
:::

:::

### Varying fill colours along the x axis

::: panel-tabset
## The plot


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Hands-on_Ex04a_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-5-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## The code chunk


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(exam,
       aes(x = ENGLISH,
           y = CLASS,
           fill = stat(x))) +
  geom_density_ridges_gradient(
    scale = 3,
    rel_min_height = 0.01) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(name = "Temp. [F]",
                       option = "C") +
  scale_x_continuous(
    name = "English grades",
    expand = c(0, 0)
  ) +
  scale_y_discrete(name = NULL, expand = expansion(add = c(0.2, 2.6))) +
  theme_ridges()
```
:::

:::

### Mapping probabilities directly onto colour

::: panel-tabset
## The plot


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Hands-on_Ex04a_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-7-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## The code chunk


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(exam,
       aes(x = ENGLISH,
           y = CLASS,
           fill = 0.5 - abs(0.5-stat(ecdf)))) +
  stat_density_ridges(geom = "density_ridges_gradient",
                      calc_ecdf = TRUE) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(name = "Tail probability",
                       direction = -1) +
  theme_ridges()
```
:::

:::

### Ridgeline plots with quantile lines

::: panel-tabset
## The plot


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Hands-on_Ex04a_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-9-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## The code chunk


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(exam,
       aes(x = ENGLISH,
           y = CLASS,
           fill = factor(stat(quantile))
           )) +
  stat_density_ridges(
    geom = "density_ridges_gradient",
    calc_ecdf = TRUE,
    quantiles = 4,
    quantile_lines = TRUE) +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(name = "Quartiles") +
  theme_ridges()
```
:::

:::

### Ridgeline plot with tail probability cut points

::: panel-tabset
## The plot


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Hands-on_Ex04a_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-11-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## The code chunk


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(exam,
       aes(x = ENGLISH,
           y = CLASS,
           fill = factor(stat(quantile))
           )) +
  stat_density_ridges(
    geom = "density_ridges_gradient",
    calc_ecdf = TRUE,
    quantiles = c(0.025, 0.975)
    ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    name = "Probability",
    values = c("#FF0000A0", "#A0A0A0A0", "#0000FFA0"),
    labels = c("(0, 0.025]", "(0.025, 0.975]", "(0.975, 1]")
  ) +
  theme_ridges()
```
:::

:::

## Visualising Distribution with Raincloud Plot

A raincloud plot combines a half-density plot, a boxplot and a dot plot — giving a much richer picture of the data distribution than a boxplot alone.

### Step 1 — Half Eye graph

::: panel-tabset
## The plot


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Hands-on_Ex04a_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-13-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## The code chunk


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
exam %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = RACE,
             y = ENGLISH)) +
  stat_halfeye(adjust = 0.5,
               justification = -0.2,
               .width = 0,
               point_colour = NA)
```
:::

:::

### Step 2 — Adding boxplot

::: panel-tabset
## The plot


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Hands-on_Ex04a_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-15-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## The code chunk


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
exam %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = RACE,
             y = ENGLISH)) +
  stat_halfeye(adjust = 0.5,
               justification = -0.2,
               .width = 0,
               point_colour = NA) +
  geom_boxplot(width = .20,
               outlier.shape = NA)
```
:::

:::

### Step 3 — Adding dot plots

::: panel-tabset
## The plot


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Hands-on_Ex04a_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-17-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## The code chunk


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
exam %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = RACE,
             y = ENGLISH)) +
  stat_halfeye(adjust = 0.5,
               justification = -0.2,
               .width = 0,
               point_colour = NA) +
  geom_boxplot(width = .20,
               outlier.shape = NA) +
  stat_dots(side = "left",
            justification = 1.2,
            binwidth = .5,
            dotsize = 2)
```
:::

:::

### Step 4 — Final raincloud plot

::: panel-tabset
## The plot


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Hands-on_Ex04a_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-19-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## The code chunk


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
exam %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = RACE,
             y = ENGLISH)) +
  stat_halfeye(adjust = 0.5,
               justification = -0.2,
               .width = 0,
               point_colour = NA) +
  geom_boxplot(width = .20,
               outlier.shape = NA) +
  stat_dots(side = "left",
            justification = 1.2,
            binwidth = .5,
            dotsize = 2) +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_economist()
```
:::

:::

## Reflections

This exercise introduced two new ways to visualise distribution that I hadn't seen before. Ridgeline plots are great for comparing distributions across many groups at once — much cleaner than stacking multiple histograms.

The raincloud plot was the most interesting — it combines three views in one chart: the density shape, the boxplot summary, and the actual data points. This gives a much more complete picture than just a boxplot alone.

The step-by-step build up of the raincloud plot (half eye → boxplot → dots → flip) was a good way to understand how each layer adds information.

## References

-   [ggridges documentation](https://wilkelab.org/ggridges/)
-   [ggdist documentation](https://mjskay.github.io/ggdist/)
-   [R for Visual Analytics Chapter 9](https://r4va.netlify.app/chap09)
